Network Pharmacology Analysis of Quercetin Against Type 2 Diabetes

Musfirah Tanveer

2026-07-09

Abstract

This study employed network pharmacology to investigate the molecular mechanisms of Quercetin against Type 2 Diabetes (T2D). A total of 272 Quercetin targets were identified from HERB and SwissTargetPrediction databases. Intersection with 40 T2D-related genes yielded 27 key targets. Hub gene analysis identified AKT1 and IL6 as the most connected genes. KEGG enrichment revealed AGE-RAGE signaling as the top pathway.

1. Introduction

Quercetin (3,3’,4’,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone) is a naturally occurring flavonoid found abundantly in fruits, vegetables, and grains. It has well-documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antidiabetic properties. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2D) affects over 500 million people worldwide and remains a major public health challenge.

Network pharmacology provides a systems-level approach to understand how natural compounds interact with multiple targets simultaneously. This study applies network pharmacology to elucidate the molecular mechanisms of Quercetin against T2D.

2. Methods

2.1 Quercetin Target Collection

Quercetin targets were collected from two databases: - HERB database (herb.ac.cn): literature-based targets - SwissTargetPrediction (swisstargetprediction.ch): structure-based predictions

2.2 T2D Disease Gene Collection

Type 2 Diabetes genes were collected from published literature.

2.3 Network Construction

Intersection targets were submitted to STRING database with confidence score > 0.700. PPI network was analyzed using igraph R package.

2.4 Enrichment Analysis

GO and KEGG pathway enrichment performed using enrichR R package.

3. Results

3.5 Hub Gene Identification

Table 2: Top 10 hub genes by degree centrality

AKT1 18 Insulin signaling kinase
IL6 18 Inflammatory cytokine
TNF 16 Inflammatory mediator
IL1B 15 Inflammasome activation
TP53 15 Cell survival regulation
SIRT1 13 Metabolic regulation
ADIPOQ 12 Adipokine signaling
BCL2 12 Apoptosis regulation

CASP3 11 Apoptosis execution

3.7 GO Biological Process Enrichment

Figure 4: GO biological process enrichment

4. Discussion

Network pharmacology analysis identified 27 key targets through which Quercetin may exert antidiabetic effects. AKT1 and IL6 emerged as the top hub genes with the highest degree centrality (degree = 18 each), suggesting Quercetin primarily modulates insulin signaling and inflammatory pathways simultaneously.

The AGE-RAGE signaling pathway in diabetic complications was the most significantly enriched KEGG pathway, directly relevant to T2D pathology. This suggests Quercetin may protect against diabetic complications through multiple molecular mechanisms.

5. Conclusion

This integrated network pharmacology analysis demonstrated that Quercetin targets T2D through 27 key genes, primarily through AKT1 and IL6 hub genes. The PI3K-Akt and AGE-RAGE signaling pathways were identified as key mechanisms, providing a systems-level understanding of Quercetin’s antidiabetic effects.

References

  1. HERB database: http://herb.ac.cn
  2. SwissTargetPrediction: http://swisstargetprediction.ch
  3. STRING database: https://string-db.org
  4. Enrichr: https://maayanlab.cloud/Enrichr/
  5. R Core Team (2024). R: A language and environment for statistical computing.
